name: Adult ADHD Self-Report Scale (Part A)
citation: Kessler, R. C., Adler, L., Ames, M., Demler, O., Faraone, S., Hiripi, E., Howes, M. J., Jin, R., Secnik, K., Spencer, T., Ustun, T. B., & Walters, E. E. (2005). The World Health Organization Adult ADHD Self-Report Scale (ASRS): a short screening scale for use in the general population. Psychological Medicine, 35(2), 245-256.
copyright: © World Health Organization 2003; © New York University and Ronald C. Kessler, PhD. All rights reserved.
validityNotes: ASRS-v1.1 Part A is the 6-item screener subset of the 18-item ASRS-v1.1 Symptom Checklist, developed jointly by the WHO Workgroup on Adult ADHD. Cutoff of 4+ shaded responses (out of 6) indicates symptoms may be consistent with adult ADHD and warrants further evaluation. Original validation showed moderate sensitivity (68.7%) and high specificity in general population samples (Kessler et al. 2005). Positive predictive value is relatively low (12-22%) in primary care, meaning a positive screen warrants follow-up rather than confirms diagnosis. Performance varies in populations with comorbid anxiety, depression, or substance use, where symptom overlap can inflate positives.
instructions: Check the box that best describes how you have felt and conducted yourself over the past 6 months.

items.text: How often do you have trouble wrapping up the final details of a project, once the challenging parts have been done?
How often do you have difficulty getting things in order when you have to do a task that requires organization?
How often do you have problems remembering appointments or obligations?
When you have a task that requires a lot of thought, how often do you avoid or delay getting started?
How often do you fidget or squirm with your hands or feet when you have to sit down for a long time?
How often do you feel overly active and compelled to do things, like you were driven by a motor?

scoring.cutoffMeaning: A score of 4 or above suggests symptoms may be consistent with adult ADHD; the original developers recommend talking with a healthcare provider about an evaluation.

fullName: Adult ADHD Self-Report Scale v1.1, Symptom Checklist Part A
